mnemonics.title: Hypothesis Function Song
mnemonics.content: H-of-x equals theta-not plus theta-one-x, we predict y with a line that fits just flex! (Repeat to the tune of 'Pop Goes the Weasel')

mnemonics.title: Minimize the Error
mnemonics.content: Square the error, sum it all, divide by 2m - that's the call! MSE guides our way, finding best fit day by day!

mnemonics.title: Gradient Descent Steps
mnemonics.content: Repeat until convergence: compute the gradient, then update theta - subtract alpha times the derivative, that's the formula!

mnemonics.title: Cost Function Purpose
mnemonics.content: The cost function's job is to penalize big mistakes - large errors become HUGE when we square them!

acronyms.usage: Cost function that averages the squared difference between predictions and actual values
Square root of MSE - gives error in same units as y
Sum of squared differences between predicted and actual values
Sum of squared differences between actual values and the mean
Measures how well the model explains variance in the data (1 = perfect, 0 = baseline)

visualTips.visualization: Draw an X-Y axis, plot data points as dots, draw a straight line cutting through them - the line equation is h(x) = θ₀ + θ₁x where θ₀ is y-intercept and θ₁ is slope
Picture a ball on a curved hill (the cost function surface) - gradient descent is the ball rolling downhill until it reaches the lowest point (global minimum)
For simple linear regression with one variable, the cost function J(θ₀,θ₁) creates a bowl-shaped 3D surface - we want to find the bottom of the bowl!
Draw three scenarios: too small α = tiny steps taking forever; too large α = overshooting and diverging; just right α = smooth convergence to minimum

analogies.analogy: Like trying to pass a straight stick through a crowd of people standing on a graph - you adjust the stick's angle and position until it gets as close as possible to everyone
Like hiking down a mountain in thick fog: you feel the slope under your feet and take steps in the direction that goes downhill most steeply - repeat until you're at the bottom
Like a golf score - you want the LOWEST score possible. Each swing (prediction) that misses the hole (actual value) adds to your score. Your goal is to minimize the total score.
Think of θ₁ as how steep a ramp is - positive θ₁ means as x increases, y increases (uphill), negative θ₁ means as x increases, y decreases (downhill)
θ₀ is like the y-intercept - where your prediction line crosses the y-axis, representing the baseline prediction when x equals zero
